nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing, highly oxygenated rocky rivers and tributaries; crevices and caves in submerged rockwork over sandy or mixed substrate

decorPreferences: smooth rockwork with crevices and small caves
purpose-made ceramic or PVC breeding caves
driftwood for shelter and biofilm grazing
strong circulation (powerhead or spray bar) for high oxygenation

diet.notes: The Columbian Zebra Pleco is a carnivore-leaning omnivore whose staple diet should consist of high-protein sinking foods: frozen bloodworm, white mosquito larvae, Mysis shrimp, chopped prawn, and sinking pellets formulated for carnivores or Loricariids. Once acclimated, it generally accepts vegetable matter as well — algae wafers, blanched zucchini, cucumber, and spirulina-based foods. Unlike many plecos, it is not an algae grazer and should not be expected to clean algae from the aquarium glass or décor. Because it is nocturnal, feeding after lights-out gives it the best opportunity to eat before more active tankmates do.

goodWith: apistogramma and small dwarf cichlids (with adequate caves)
avoidWith: other Hypancistrus or Loricariid plecos (cave competition and possible hybridisation with L340/L454/L474 variants)
boisterous or fast-feeding mid-level cichlids that outcompete at feeding time
any species intolerant of warm (26–30°C), fast-flowing, well-oxygenated water

summary: The Columbian Zebra Pleco (Hypancistrus debilittera, L129) is a small, carnivore-leaning catfish native to the lower Orinoco system in Colombia. Reaching around 10 cm, it is widely captive-bred and serves as a more accessible alternative to the export-restricted Brazilian Zebra Pleco. It requires warm, fast-flowing, well-oxygenated water and is considered intermediate in difficulty.
careNotes:
The Columbian Zebra Pleco is collected primarily from the Rio Bita, a tributary of the Rio Meta within the Orinoco drainage on the Colombia–Venezuela border, which places it as the northernmost-ranging Hypancistrus in South America. It should not be confused with the true Zebra Pleco (L046) from the Rio Xingu in Brazil — that species, Hypancistrus zebra, is subject to a Brazilian export ban and commands significantly higher prices. The L129 is widely captive-bred and is comparably tractable for intermediate hobbyists.

Water quality and oxygenation are the most critical parameters. The species requires warm water (23–30°C, ideally 26–28°C for long-term maintenance), soft to moderately hard, slightly acidic to neutral conditions, with strong flow and high dissolved oxygen. A powerhead or spray bar directed across cave entrances closely mimics the fast-moving, rocky riverine habitat from which this fish comes. Robust mechanical and biological filtration is essential, as these fish produce significant waste relative to their size. Weekly water changes of 25–30% are strongly recommended.

Despite being a pleco, the L129 is not an algae grazer — do not rely on it to clean the aquarium. It is carnivore-leaning and does best on protein-rich sinking foods offered after lights-out. Meaty frozen foods such as bloodworm, Mysis shrimp, and prawn pieces should form the bulk of the diet, supplemented by quality sinking pellets formulated for Loricariids. Occasional vegetable matter such as blanched zucchini or cucumber is generally accepted and beneficial once the fish is settled.

Multiple caves — ceramic tubes, PVC pipes, or purpose-made Loricariid spawning caves — should be provided even for a single specimen, as this species is strongly cave-reliant for shelter and security. Males may quarrel over prime caves; keeping one male with two or three females in an appropriately sized tank with surplus caves generally reduces aggression. Only one Hypancistrus species should be kept per tank to prevent hybridisation with morphologically similar forms such as L340, L454, and L474.

Sexing becomes easier in mature adults: males develop longer odontodes (bristle-like spines) on the pectoral fin rays and cheeks and tend to have broader, flatter heads, while females are rounder in the belly. See also: [zebra-pleco] — the Brazilian congener; Brazilian export restrictions make wild-caught L046 extremely rare and expensive.
breedingNotes: The Columbian Zebra Pleco is a cave spawner and is considered moderately difficult to breed. Conditioning adults on a varied high-protein diet is the recommended starting point; provide one small ceramic or PVC cave per fish, with an interior diameter just wide enough for the male to turn around. Raising the temperature to 28–30°C and directing a powerhead current across cave entrances tends to trigger spawning behaviour. The male guards the clutch of roughly 15–25 eggs inside the cave, and the eggs typically hatch in approximately 5–7 days. Fry remain in the cave consuming their yolk sac for a further week before becoming free-swimming. First foods should be newly hatched brine shrimp, micro-worm, and finely crushed carnivore pellet. Excellent water quality must be maintained throughout, as fry are sensitive to ammonia spikes. A separate breeding or grow-out tank generally improves fry survival rates. Captive-bred specimens are available from specialty aquarium retailers and hobbyist breeders.

primary.notes: Primary husbandry reference. Provided adult size (7–8 cm TL), temperature (23–28°C, breeding 28–30°C), pH (5.5–7.5), GH (2–15 dH), native range (Rio Bita tributary of Rio Orinoco, upstream from Puerto Careño, Colombia/Venezuela), diet (primarily carnivorous; protein-rich staples plus vegetable matter when acclimated), tank dimensions (80×35 cm minimum; 100×40 cm for groups), breeding notes (cave spawner, 2–3 females per male ratio), and behaviour notes.

additional.name: Catfish Codex — Hypancistrus debilittera L129

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping (Fishkeeper.co.uk) — Colombian Zebra Plec L129
additional.notes: Corroborated temperature (22–30°C), pH (5.5–7.2), hardness (up to 15 KH), native range (Venezuela and Colombia, Orinoco River basin), tank requirements (brisk water movement, oxygenation, sand substrate, rocky décor), diet (bloodworm, mosquito larvae, Mysis shrimp, chopped prawns, carnivore sinking pellets), and egg/breeding data (20–30 eggs, 5–7 day hatch).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Hypancistrus debilittera
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes, Ancistrin tribe within Hypostominae subfamily), described by Armbruster, Lujan & Taphorn 2007, native range (Venezuela and Colombia, Orinoco River system), IUCN Least Concern.

fish.breedingNotes: The Columbian Zebra Pleco spawns in caves, with the male guarding a clutch of roughly 15–25 eggs until they hatch in approximately 5–7 days. Spawning can generally be triggered by raising the temperature to 28–30°C, directing strong flow across cave entrances, and conditioning adults on a protein-rich diet. Fry accept newly hatched brine shrimp and micro-worm as first foods. Captive-bred specimens are available from specialty aquarium retailers and hobbyist breeders.
